name: South Central
bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring (March-May) or autumn (September-November) to avoid hot, humid summers.

places.name: Guangzhou
places.description: The capital of Guangdong province and a major economic hub, Guangzhou blends ultra-modern skylines with ancient temples and excellent Cantonese cuisine. It's essential for experiencing contemporary China while maintaining deep historical roots along the Pearl River.

activities.detail: This 600-meter TV tower offers panoramic views of the Pearl River and city skyline from its observation decks and rotating restaurant.

activities.text: Temple of the Six Banyan Trees
activities.detail: An ancient Buddhist temple dating to 537 CE featuring a striking nine-story pagoda and intricate wooden carvings in the heart of the old city.
A masterpiece of Cantonese folk architecture showcasing traditional craftsmanship with detailed carvings, sculptures, and now home to a folk art museum.
Evening boat tours along the illuminated Pearl River revealing the city's modern skyline contrasted with historic waterfront areas and traditional villages.
A serene urban park with scenic lakes, traditional pavilions, and walking paths offering respite from the bustling city center.
Expansive gardens featuring rare tropical plants, traditional Chinese landscape design, and peaceful trails through diverse ecosystems.

food.detail: The iconic Cantonese dining tradition of small steamed and fried dumplings, rolls, and pastries served with tea, best experienced in traditional tea houses during morning hours.
Succulent whole goose roasted until the skin is crispy and mahogany-colored, served with sweet dipping sauce and rice, a Cantonese specialty found in dedicated roasteries.

food.text: Congee with Century Egg and Pork
food.detail: Savory rice porridge topped with preserved century egg, salted pork, and fresh scallions, commonly eaten for breakfast or as comfort food.
Tiny freshwater snails extracted from their shells with a toothpick and served in fragrant herbal or spiced broths, a beloved street food and specialty.
Delicate shrimp and pork wontons served in silky egg noodles swimming in savory broth, a quick meal available at noodle shops throughout the city.

culture.detail: While Mandarin Chinese is understood, Cantonese is the predominant spoken language; learning basic Cantonese greetings shows respect and aids connection with locals.
When visiting temples, dress modestly, remove shoes where indicated, and avoid touching religious artifacts; photography may be restricted in certain areas.
At street markets and smaller vendors, polite negotiation over prices is customary and often expected, though prices in malls and chain stores are fixed.
If conducting business, exchange cards with both hands and examine any received card respectfully before placing it on the table, never write on someone's card.

places.name: Shenzhen
places.description: A former fishing village transformed into China's tech and innovation capital, Shenzhen epitomizes rapid modernization with cutting-edge architecture and vibrant culture. The city offers world-class shopping, dining, and a gateway to Hong Kong just across the border.

activities.detail: A theme park featuring miniature replicas of famous Chinese landmarks and cultural attractions across 30 hectares of landscaped gardens.
An amusement park with scale models of world monuments including the Eiffel Tower, Statue of Liberty, and Egyptian pyramids, plus performance shows.
The world's largest electronics wholesale market sprawling across multiple buildings where you can source gadgets, components, and tech at factory prices.
An artistic enclave in southern Shenzhen where hundreds of studios produce and sell hand-painted reproductions of famous artworks and original oil paintings.
A creative industry park housed in a former industrial warehouse complex featuring art galleries, boutique shops, cafes, and cultural venues.
A popular coastal beach in the eastern part of the city perfect for swimming, water sports, and enjoying seafood restaurants with ocean views.
A comprehensive museum documenting the city's transformation from fishing village to tech metropolis with exhibitions on history, art, and contemporary culture.

food.detail: Cantonese dim sum served cart-style in restaurants throughout the city, featuring har gow, siu mai, and other delicate steamed and fried dumplings.
A dining experience where raw seafood, vegetables, and noodles are cooked tableside in aromatic broths, especially popular in restaurants near Meisha Beach.
A traditional Cantonese winter delicacy made with shredded snake meat, medicinal herbs, and mushrooms, served hot in ceramic bowls.
Succulent Cantonese-style roasted goose with crispy skin and tender meat, typically served sliced with a savory dipping sauce.
Creamy rice porridge served with century egg, pork, and pickled vegetables, eaten as a comforting breakfast or light meal at dedicated congee restaurants.
Poached or steamed chicken from nearby Qingyuan city served with ginger-scallion dipping sauce, reflecting the region's strong culinary connection to Guangdong cuisine.

culture.detail: Shenzhen is almost entirely cashless; use WeChat Pay or Alipay for nearly all transactions from taxis to street food vendors to avoid carrying cash.
While the city moves fast, arriving on time for meetings and reservations is important; Shenzhen's tech culture values efficiency and professionalism.
English is less prevalent than in Hong Kong; learning simple phrases helps with navigation and dining, and locals appreciate the effort to speak Mandarin or Cantonese.
Major markets and outdoor attractions are best experienced during daylight hours; many shops close by 10 PM, making early morning exploration ideal for authentic experiences.

places.name: Zhangjiajie
places.description: Famous for its towering sandstone pillars that inspired Avatar's landscape, Zhangjiajie National Forest Park is one of China's most spectacular natural wonders. Visitors can hike through mist-shrouded mountains, cross glass bridges, and witness breathtaking panoramic views.

activities.detail: Ride the world's longest cable car up Tianmen Mountain for panoramic views of the city and surrounding peaks.
Walk across a thrilling transparent glass pathway suspended over a 1,430-meter drop for an adrenaline-pumping experience.
Cross the world's longest and highest glass-bottomed bridge spanning 430 meters above a canyon, offering stunning valley views.
Trek through the iconic sandstone pillar forest that inspired Avatar's Pandora landscape along the scenic Golden Whip Stream trail.
Stroll through a picturesque valley corridor featuring towering rock formations and mist-covered peaks that showcase the park's natural artistry.
Glide across a serene alpine lake surrounded by dramatic cliffs and limestone formations, accessible by cable car or hiking.
Wake early to witness the mist-shrouded mountains emerge with golden light from above the clouds at this elevated vantage point.

food.detail: A savory traditional Tujia ethnic drink made with roasted tea leaves, peanuts, and sesame oil served as a warming breakfast staple.
Locally cured and smoked pork that reflects the region's preservation traditions, often served with preserved vegetables at local restaurants.
Silky rice noodles served in a flavorful broth with local vegetables and spiced meat, representing the comfort food of western Hunan province.

food.text: Tujia Sticky Rice Balls (土家糍粑)
food.detail: Chewy glutinous rice cakes fried until crispy on the outside, traditionally served with sweet or savory toppings as a festive treat.

food.text: Wuling Mountain Spring Water Fish (武陵山泉水鱼)
food.detail: Fresh local fish cooked in the region's pure mountain spring water, often prepared with wild herbs and local chili peppers for authentic flavor.

culture.detail: Zhangjiajie is home to the Tujia ethnic minority; showing respect for their traditions, festivals, and local customs enriches your visit and supports community pride.
The famous misty mountains are weather-dependent and the park attracts significant crowds year-round; visit early morning or in shoulder seasons for the best experience.
Trails are steep and crowded; stay on marked paths, move aside for others, and carry sufficient water as the distances are longer than they appear.
Local markets and vendor stalls expect friendly negotiation on prices, but maintain courtesy and cultural sensitivity when purchasing souvenirs or provisions.

places.name: Wuhan
places.description: A major transportation and cultural hub at the confluence of the Yangtze and Han rivers, Wuhan is known for its riverside promenades, historic architecture, and excellent museums. The city represents central China's rising importance and offers authentic local culture.

activities.detail: Iconic 5-story pagoda offering panoramic views of the Yangtze River and city skyline from its riverside perch.
Scenic waterfront walkway lined with restored colonial architecture, parks, and river views perfect for evening strolls.
World-class museum housing ancient bronze bells, jade artifacts, and relics from the Kingdom of Chu with excellent English signage.
Large urban lake with walking and cycling paths, lakeside gardens, and temples offering peaceful respite from city bustle.
Modern museum showcasing the city's 3,500-year history with exhibits on local culture, urban development, and regional art.
Historic riverside pavilion offering sunset views and access to traditional tea houses along the Han River.

food.detail: Wuhan's signature street dish of sesame-flavored noodles served cold with pickled vegetables and a rich sauce, found everywhere from stalls to restaurants.
Spicy braised duck necks marinated in chili and Sichuan peppercorns, sold as a popular snack and take-away item throughout the city.
Soft tofu skins rolled with glutinous rice and meat, steamed and served warm, a beloved breakfast item in local dim sum.
Delicate broth made from fresh Yangtze river fish and shrimp, typically served with Chinese chives and enjoyed as a light local specialty.
Sweet sticky rice flour balls filled with sesame or peanut paste, commonly served as dessert or at night markets.

culture.detail: The Yangtze and Han rivers are central to Wuhan's identity; locals enjoy early morning tai chi and riverside gatherings, so joining these activities shows cultural appreciation.
Popular breakfast and snack vendors sell out by mid-morning, so visit street food markets between 6 and 8 AM for the best selection and authentic local experience.
The rapid metro system with English signage is the fastest way to navigate; taxis and ride-sharing are also reliable but traffic can be heavy during rush hours.
Wuhan hosts spring cherry blossom festivals and summer riverside events; timing your visit around these celebrations provides deeper cultural immersion and festive atmosphere.

places.name: Changsha
places.description: The capital of Hunan province on the Xiang River, Changsha is a dynamic city famous for its street food culture and historical significance in Chinese revolution. It offers excellent regional cuisine, cultural museums, and riverside development.
imageQueries: Changsha Xiang River tower city lights

activities.detail: A scenic riverside park on the Xiang River with walking paths, sculptures, and views commemorating Mao Zedong's 1925 visit.
Houses the famous Mawangdui Han Dynasty silk manuscripts and lacquerware, essential for understanding Hunan's ancient history.
Historic campus where Mao Zedong studied and taught, featuring restored buildings and revolutionary history exhibits.
Ancient tower overlooking the city with a small museum documenting Changsha's role in the 1911 revolution.
Historic pedestrian alley packed with traditional snack stalls, tea houses, and local food vendors representing Changsha's famous street food culture.
Walk the newly developed riverside promenade at dusk to see the city lights reflect off the water and local residents enjoying outdoor activities.

food.detail: Fermented tofu blocks deep-fried until crispy outside and creamy inside, served with chili sauce—a Changsha specialty that challenges and delights adventurous eaters.
Fresh river shrimp stir-fried with fiery local chilies, garlic, and Sichuan peppercorns, embodying Changsha's bold and spicy culinary tradition.
Delicate steamed rolls of thin rice noodles wrapped around pork, shrimp, or vegetables, typically eaten for breakfast with soy sauce and chili oil.
A famous local snack of glutinous rice balls filled with sesame and peanut paste, sold at street stalls throughout the city.
Cubed tofu in a numbing and spicy sauce with minced pork, representing the numbing-spicy (málà) flavor profile central to Hunan cuisine.

culture.detail: Changsha locals take pride in their love of chili peppers; most dishes are genuinely spicy, so specify your heat preference clearly or request reduced chili.
Popular breakfast and snack vendors on Pozi Street and night markets operate early morning and evening; arrive before 9 AM or after 5 PM for the best experience.
Changsha holds deep significance in Chinese communist history; approach museums and monuments with respect, and avoid dismissive comments about historical figures.
While Mandarin is widely spoken, older vendors and locals in traditional areas speak Changsha dialect (长沙话); a few polite greetings endear you to locals.

places.name: Xiangxi (Phoenix & Fenghuang)
places.description: An ethnic minority region featuring the ancient town of Fenghuang with traditional wooden buildings perched above turquoise rivers, Xiangxi preserves authentic Miao and Tujia cultures. The dramatic landscape and preserved heritage make it one of China's most evocative regions.

activities.detail: Stroll through narrow cobblestone streets lined with traditional wooden buildings that overhang the Tuojiang River, browsing local shops and soaking in the lantern-lit atmosphere.
Paddle through turquoise waters beneath limestone cliffs and past wooden stilt houses, stopping at scenic viewpoints and small villages along the way.
Hike to this fortified Miao settlement perched on a mountain ridge to experience traditional architecture and panoramic views of the surrounding valleys.
Spend time in authentic Miao villages like Xiaoxi or Guzhai to witness traditional textile weaving, embroidery work, and daily life of the ethnic minority community.
Walk along the Ming Dynasty-era defensive walls that encircle Fenghuang, offering elevated views of the town and river below.
Trek to this lesser-known hillside settlement famous for ancient Tujia wooden structures and traditional craftsmanship away from the main tourist crowds.
Experience the lively evening markets in Fenghuang where vendors sell street snacks, grilled meats, and local specialties amid crowds of locals and travelers.

food.detail: A signature regional dish featuring fresh river fish in a tangy broth flavored with sour plums, chilies, and local herbs that balances heat and sourness.
Crispy fried pancakes filled with savory ingredients like wild vegetables, cured meat, or cheese, traditionally made by Tujia families and sold at local markets.
A spicy Hunan specialty where duck is cooked with its blood, fresh chilies, and pickled vegetables for a rich and intensely flavored dish.
Tender freshwater snails grilled with garlic, chilies, and local herbs, offering a chewy texture and authentic taste of the river ecosystem.
Glutinous rice cakes made with local herbs, bamboo leaves, and sometimes filled with red bean or Chinese sausage, sold by Miao vendors in the town.
Local preservation specialty where wild ginger is candied and dried, creating a sweet-spicy snack popular among both locals and tourists in Fenghuang.

culture.detail: Always ask permission before photographing local residents, especially in minority villages where some people may find it intrusive or have spiritual concerns about images.

culture.text: Learn Basic Greetings in Local Languages
culture.detail: While Mandarin is spoken, learning a few words in Miao or Tujia language shows respect and is deeply appreciated by ethnic minority residents.
When visiting village temples, ancestral halls, or participating in local ceremonies, wear long pants and covered shoulders as a sign of respect for cultural and spiritual traditions.

If visiting during Miao New Year (typically January-February) or Tujia festivals, embrace the celebrations respectfully and understand that these are meaningful cultural events, not just tourist attractions.

places.name: Chongqing
places.description: A sprawling mountain city on the Yangtze River, Chongqing is known for its spicy cuisine, dramatic topography, and as a gateway to the scenic Three Gorges. The city's modern development contrasts with ancient temples and natural attractions.

activities.detail: An ancient riverside cave complex carved into the cliff face, now a multi-level shopping and entertainment venue with stunning night views of the Yangtze River.
Visit the world's largest hydroelectric dam located 40 km downstream, accessible via organized tours that include the visitor center and ship lift.
UNESCO-listed ancient Buddhist and Taoist stone sculptures carved into cliffs, dating back over 1,000 years, located about 160 km from the city center.

activities.text: Chongqing Cable Car over the Yangtze
activities.detail: The historic aerial tramway spanning the Yangtze River between Jiaochangba and Longmenhao offers panoramic city and river views.
A sanctuary about 100 km away dedicated to giant panda conservation and breeding, where visitors can observe and learn about these endangered animals.
An iconic obelisk in the heart of downtown Jiefangbei standing 27.5 meters tall, symbolizing the city's liberation and surrounded by shops and restaurants.
A historic park perched on a mountain offering 360-degree panoramic views of the city and the confluence of the Yangtze and Jialing rivers.

food.detail: A communal dining experience where diners cook raw ingredients in a spicy broth infused with chili peppers and Sichuan peppercorns at the table.
Steamed pork dumplings with soup inside, though Chongqing's version often features spicy dipping sauces and bold flavors distinct from Shanghai originals.
A spicy street food where customers select raw vegetables, tofu, and meats on sticks, which are then boiled in a fiery chili and numbing Sichuan peppercorn broth.
Diced chicken tossed with whole dried chili peppers, Sichuan peppercorns, and aromatics, creating a vibrant, intensely spicy and numbing dish.
A breakfast noodle soup made with thin rice or wheat noodles served in a spicy broth topped with scallions, peanuts, and bean sprouts.
Fresh river fish braised with pickled mustard greens and fermented bean paste, a local specialty reflecting the city's Yangtze riverside heritage.

culture.detail: Chongqing is renowned for fiery cuisine; most local dishes feature chili peppers and Sichuan peppercorns, so inquire about spice levels and come prepared for heat.
The city's steep topography means many neighborhoods are connected by stairs and narrow alleys rather than flat streets; wear comfortable walking shoes and expect vertical navigation.

culture.text: Respect Local Customs at Hot Pot
culture.detail: When dining at hot pot, never leave the communal serving utensils in the broth for extended periods and return them to their original position to maintain etiquette.
While Mandarin is widely spoken, locals use distinct Chongqing dialect words; learning a few terms like 'chi le mei you' (have you eaten) shows respect for local culture.

restaurants.name: Juyixuan
restaurants.description: Legendary Hunan restaurant famous for authentic spicy dishes and preserved vegetables that define the region's bold flavors.

restaurants.name: Fenghuang Xiangcun
restaurants.description: Casual riverside eatery in an ancient town serving traditional Miao and Tujia minority cuisine in rustic surroundings.

restaurants.name: Yuelu Shanzhuang
restaurants.description: Upscale restaurant near Yuelu Mountain specializing in game meats and wild herbs prepared in traditional Hunan style.

restaurants.name: Lao Han's Noodle House
restaurants.description: No-frills noodle shop beloved for authentic fried bean sprouts and hot noodles that encapsulate Wuhan's casual dining culture.

restaurants.name: Bao'ensi Vegetarian Restaurant
restaurants.description: Elegant Buddhist vegetarian establishment creating sophisticated meat-free dishes using innovative plant-based techniques.
restaurants.mustOrder: Mock Peking Duck with Wheat Gluten

restaurants.name: Duyun Suiyuan
restaurants.description: Contemporary Guizhou restaurant highlighting the province's sour and spicy preparations with locally foraged ingredients.

restaurants.name: Kaili Folk Village Restaurant
restaurants.description: Family-run restaurant serving authentic Miao ethnic food with traditional fermented ingredients and tribal cooking methods.
restaurants.mustOrder: Glutinous Rice with Miao Pickled Vegetables

restaurants.name: Lushan Restaurant
restaurants.description: Historic mountain restaurant with views serving Jiangxi specialties including fresh bamboo shoots and wild mushrooms.

restaurants.name: Jingdezhen Porcelain Tea House Restaurant
restaurants.description: Artistic dining space celebrating Jingdezhen's pottery heritage while serving refined Jiangxi cuisine and fine teas.
restaurants.mustOrder: Steamed Local Fish with Porcelain-served Sauces

restaurants.name: Chefs' Table Zhangjiajie
restaurants.description: Fine dining establishment with dramatic scenic views offering contemporary interpretations of traditional Hunan mountain cuisine.
restaurants.mustOrder: Smoked Local Chicken with Zhangjiajie Mushrooms

restaurants.name: Louyang's Home Cooking
restaurants.description: Rustic family restaurant serving unpretentious country-style Hunan dishes in a traditional countryside setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Stewed Pork with Dried Chilies and Preserved Radish

restaurants.name: Yangshuo West Street Café & Restaurant
restaurants.description: Bohemian restaurant blending Western comfort food with Guilin-Yangshuo local specialties in a touristy but quality setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Bamboo Raft Fish with Fresh River Vegetables

restaurants.name: Guilin Restaurant Old Brand
restaurants.description: Time-honored establishment perfecting Guilin's famous rice noodle soups and local protein dishes for five generations.
restaurants.mustOrder: Guilin Rice Noodles with Braised Meat

restaurants.name: Wuxi Zhu Hongxing Spare Ribs
restaurants.description: Iconic restaurant famous throughout China for its signature braised spare ribs using a proprietary sauce perfected over decades.

restaurants.name: Nanxiang Steamed Bun Restaurant
restaurants.description: Legendary dumpling house with multiple Shanghai locations serving the city's most famous xiaolongbao with pork and crab roe.
